sentences.text: Well, I just want to add that I tried to find her after the war was over.
And closest I came is to find that she was deported from Poznan as a slave laborer, to some city.
They thought it was Dresden.
Dresden was one of those cities was very heavily bombarded in the last months of the war.
And she never came back, nor have-- has their family-- could I found their family.
I did not go back to Poznan personally, because of what happened in Kielce at the time.
As you well probably know, they were 70 Jews who returned to Kielce that were slaughtered, after the war, after the Holocaust, after so many have lost their lives, just because they were Jews.
Oh, Louise, we talked about what a young man and a young boy and a young girl would talk about.
We talked about American movies.
We talked about school.
We talked about books.
We talked about life.
We talked about each one about him-- his own life, to one another, and what it was like, and what they experience, and what they knew.
How their life was.
And people that are in love don't need to have a special subject to talk about.
They just can say hello and how are you and I love you and this and that.
And that is just so precious and so ample that you don't need to discuss things.
She has told me that she had a Jewish music teacher and she took piano lessons.
And she loved them very much.
She-- that's the only Jew that she knew, by the way.
And that was about what we could talk about.
Nothing-- nothing special.
Well, I ought to talk about how our people were treated and how hard they worked and how many lost their life in the bogs.
Especially when winter came, the winter of 1941 and '42, when they were depleted, they were weak.
And oftentimes just able to barely get to work.
When they were put into these difficult places to push [INAUDIBLE] into shovel, sand and soil.
And each one was 18 to 20 pounds of weight.
No less, because if you took less than a full shovel, you were mistreated.
And they did it till they fell.
And when they fell, we carried them back in stretchers.
No one has ever been revived.
There was no medical personnel in the camp to know how to revive one.
They just were left in the first aid room till they died.
And so when we were transferred to Gutenbrunn in 1942, when I escaped the threatened hanging by Krusche, we have lost approximately 20% of [? all ?] the people that we had.
And they were replaced with new prisoners, with new people brought from other ghettos.
Didn't matter.
All they needed is a phone call.
And there would be as many Jews as the Lager Furher wanted.
This wasn't about work as much as it was about the first level of destruction.
Yes.
Well, practice would be a little overstated.
I was available.
I did not officially have any excuse from not working.
I had to go every day to work.
But after work, when I came to camp, and someone needed a dentist, I was called.
And I was just limited to extractions, because I had no-- nothing else to do.
Only on inmates in the first camp.
Only on inmates.
OK.
The circumstances were such.
After Krusche has punished me for buying bread, he demoted me.
I was no longer the camp-- the commando leader.
Someone else was appointed to be the leader and I was to work as hard as anybody.
As a matter of fact, he insisted at the [NON-ENGLISH] that the foreman would put me to the worse work in that camp, so that I would [NON-ENGLISH], so that I would expire.
Krusche was the commandant.
However, the foreman, the head foreman for whom I worked in-- in the office, didn't want to take his-- his order.
And he said, as far as the camp is concerned, he can do whatever he wants with you.
But when you're on-site, come here, you work for me.
And I am not going to take his orders.
You're going to work in the office as you are.
And I was sort of like a little slice of salami between two loaves of bread.
They were two people of power vying about what to do with the Jew.
And I knew that I am in the middle, then eventually I will pay the price.
So one day when I came back, he said to me, you will be the first one to hang in this camp.
I will see to it that you hang here.
No sooner did it-- was he proclaiming that, a few days later, I came back and I saw a gallows erected in the camp.
And I knew, and I feared, that he is going to fulfill his threat.
And so, shortly thereafter, there was a group of 100 that is to leave the camp, to go to a newly established camp nearby, about 20 kilometers away.
And I ask Chaim, who was, after all, some friend of my parents', whether he could put me in in that group, put my name on.
He was a policeman in camp.
Was camp police.
And he-- he said that he will try to.
He didn't promise me.
But he will try to put my father's name and my name in.
And so on the morning that these [? hundred ?] inmates were being assembled, my name and the name of my father were among.
And I stood in line behind, hiding behind, in the early morning, behind other inmates so that he would not spot me.
Because I knew once he spotted me he would take me out.
And I succeeded to leave the camp with my father.
And a big, big, big boulder fell off my mind and off my heart when I left that camp.
There is another fortunate incident that accounts for my survival.
And so we walked to Gutenbrunn, the second camp.
That was also near Poznan.
This was a large landholdings domain.
And we were housed in wooden-- in-- in brick stables that he kept his cows.
And this was a larger camp with twice the amount of inmates and much more organized than Steineck was.
There was a first aid room.
There was a doctor.
There was a Jewish commandant.
Yes.
His name was Kurt Goldberg.
He was from Hamburg.
And he was the head of the--
The-- he was the Yiddish commandant.
The commandant.
The-- the Lager Altester.
Unlike the Lager Furher-- the Lager Furher, the leader of the camp, is an SS man.
But the Lager Altester was a-- a inmate.
Well, needless to say that he was as bad as an SS man.
They didn't have to do anything.
He did it all for them.
He was, however, helpful to me.
When I told him-- when he saw my little satchel with the instruments, my little box, he asked me what I have in there.
And I told him, those are dental instruments.
He says, who allowed you to bring those dental instruments here?
What dental instruments, what?
And I told him that they might be helpful because I had them in Steineck and I was able to help other inmates.
So maybe I will be able to help some here.
And he just let it be.
And then he came and he occupied a spot right next to me.
For some reason, he came from somewhere else and occupy the spot like that.
At that point, I found that for the first time that he is Jewish.
Because he was-- spoke German just like a Nazi.
Used the same language, the same words, the same pronunciation, the same tone.
And so I decided I won't be afraid.
He is just an inmate like I am.
I won't be afraid of him.
My father was next to me.
And he wasn't threatening me any-- any way, but just started to talk to me like one inmate to another.
And then later on, when he-- I ask him whether I could go in to see the doctor and maybe offer my services to the first aid room, to the hospital-- was already a small hospital-- he said yes.
And he gave me the doctor's name and told me I should tell them that he send me.

And so when the doctor heard that I had my own instruments and just what I did in my first camp, in the first camp that I was in, Steineck, he agreed that I could come and after work I could do what I did in Steineck.
And so I began to do that.
But in time, there were more work than it was in Steineck, twice as much.
And also, he needed an assist, he needed some help for first aid.
And he asked the commandant whether he would allow me-- him to take me in full-time.
And so I was full-time dentist and first aid help.
Another break.
And we spent from May through next April, this camp was-- also give me the opportunity to, during the day, at first, to work for a engineer of the [NON-ENGLISH].
Workplace.
And he also put me in the office, to draft things for him, to make copies of things that he has drafted, and to just generally work.
And he was a German member of the NSDAP, Nationalsozialistische Deutsche Arbeiterpartei, which is the Nazi party.
But somehow, he was a human being, too.
His name was-- it escapes me.
I will give it to you later.
And I asked him, I-- when winter came, I ask him to-- our people were so poorly clad and cold.
And I ask him whether we couldn't make a fire on the [NON-ENGLISH], keep a fire going so they could come in when they are frozen, their hands are frozen.
They were carrying rails, iron rails, in wagons, push cars, with dirt and ties for the rails.
Whether they-- he couldn't allow to have a little fire, so they could come in and warm up for a few minutes and go back to work.
You would get much more-- you had to approach him in such a way, you would get much more work out of them when they can work, rather than stay around.
And so he said yes, I could do it.
And I said, we could even brew some coffee.
And maybe they could have a little coffee while they come over.
And he brought coffee.
And he made my father-- he put my father in charge.
And he became known as the coffee man.
And he was brewing coffee there.
And he was being taken out of this hard work that we had there.
I think this was probably one contribution that I could make to many lives.
I don't know how many, and how many survived what ensued after this.
But at least for that period of time.
This was a better organized, a more orderly, but also, as time went on, the-- the treatment of Jews in camp increased to a worse and worse level from day to day.
In this camp, real hangings took place.
Every Thursday.
If it wasn't someone from our camp, they brought in someone from another camp.
They was a gallows when we came.
And that gallows was used for this.
Then one day-- we all had to stay in line.
We all had to stay and watch these hangings when we came from work.
And I can't forget one incident when one SS man of a high rank was walking with his coat over his shoulders.
And several were around him to just inspect us, so to speak.
And if someone didn't have his buttons closed, or so, he was receiving lashes, receiving-- face beaten.
And when he came by us, where I was standing, he struck me so much as a Jew, I said to the one that was standing next to me, I [? edged ?] him with my elbow.
I say, [NON-ENGLISH].
He looks like a Jew.
And he understood.
And he turned back to me.
And he opened the briefcase, in which he had several ropes-- nooses-- and he asked me, do you know what this is?
I didn't know what to say.
I knew what it was.
I knew what it meant.
But I couldn't bring that word, nooses, through my mouth.
So, I said to him, those are ropes.
He says, no.
Those are [? tzitzits, ?] which are the fringes on the Jewish tallit, Jewish shawl, prayer shawl.
And then as he walked by, the last SS man hit me with his glove in my face.
And he said, don't you know-- keep your mouth shut.
Don't you know who this is?
This is Colonel Eichmann.

He was present during this eight-man execution at the gallows for next to no crime-- for stealing a few potatoes, for being accused of trying to escape, for just made up kind of assertions that were untrue.
And I remember one man who was about to be hung, his outcry, you murderers.
You will pay for this.
You haven't won the war yet.
You will come to pay for this.
And they quickly hung him.
Their faces fell to the ground.
And we were standing in our hearts uplifted a little bit by the fact that someone who is just about pay the supreme price has the chutzpah to say that to a Nazi, to an SS man.
That was a terrible, terrible evening for me.
I couldn't go back to the barrack and take the soup and eat it.
It just wouldn't taste.
Just was too much.
We remained in Gutenbrunn till August, early August 1943.
Something very important happened.
Zosia one day brought me a letter from my sister.
And she wrote that the ghetto in Dobra is being liquidated and they are going to be deported to Chelmno, which was only about 25 miles-- 40 kilometer-- from Dobra, quite close by.
And that I should not write anymore because nobody has ever come back from Chelmno, and that when I get this letter, they will be dead.
And mother inserted a few words to try to see that Papa, Josek and I survive.
And this [? spawned ?] me to great pain, great trepidation, and my father as well.
We couldn't cry.
I had no tears.
Neither did my father.
We just knew that a Jewish life is so cheap.
And we couldn't share it with anybody else, because everybody had his own troubles to mourn.
And then shortly thereafter, I heard that they brought women to nearby and established a women's camp from our area.
And I ask [?
Tadeusz, ?] the head guard who came from Steineck with us to Gutenbrunn, whether he would take me there some night that I could go in and see whether perhaps my sister was wrong.
Perhaps she survived.
She was young.
She was capable.
She was attractive.
Maybe they let her survive.
Maybe even she would save my mother and they would both be there.
And then one night, he agreed to take me.
And he took me there.
I must say that I was extremely, extremely thankful to him for doing this, because he himself put himself in jeopardy.
He organized a bicycle for me and we both went on bicycles to this camp.
And I went from barrack to barrack at night, like a church mouse, running in the dark spots where the lights would not catch me.
And go into the barracks and keep my finger on their lips so the girls would not scream, and tell them who I am and who I'm looking for.
Until I found my cousin, Balcia, who now lives in Toronto.
And I just spoke with her the other day.
She told me not to look around.
That my mother and my sister, as did her mother and her sisters, and one of her brothers, were deported to Chelmno.
And there is no hope that I will ever see them again.
She was right.
And when my mother reminded us at our deportation that we should come back here and meet all together, I thought of [? their ?] saying, when this is all over, we'll all meet here.
We never met there again.
And so in early August, we had word about Treblinka, and Chelmno, and the Warsaw ghetto, and the uprising in the ghetto.
And we heard all these things from a resupply of inmates from the ghettos.
But the story that they just took people in forests and just machine gunned them to death was very hard to believe.
Some of us didn't believe it, including myself.
We couldn't possibly think that some element of people, who even if they hate Jews, could be that vengeful for no reason, really.
Political as it may have been, and whatever Hitler has accused us of, that they could do this, it was hard to believe.

And hard to believe was also the fact as I later found out, that my mother and my sister were asphyxiated in a truck, in one of those closed-body trucks like a UPS truck, in which the carbon monoxide was diverted inside the car.
So when they arrived in Chelmno, they were dead.
And a group of Jews that were kept in Chelmno for the purpose of cremating them, cremated them, spread their ashes through the woods of Chelmno.
I have been to Chelmno in 1985, before I wrote my book.
Before I wrote my memoir.
And I saw the inscription which proclaimed that 400,000 Jews were turned into ashes there.
And I also saw the monument that is erected on which it says, in Polish, [POLISH], which means we remember.
And on the other side of the monument, the words that they found written in blood inside at liberation-- that we are here.
We're being shot.
We're being gassed.
We're being turned into ashes.
And we are only here to destroy the bodies.
We too will be dead at the end of the war.
I must say that this moved me more than Auschwitz, moved me more than anything that I have seen on my 1985 trip to Poland, Romania, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, and France.
I think this was so [? desolate ?] and so godforsaken and so morbid that it was hard to take.
I turned away with my memories that still remain, to this day, inseparable.
Because they hurt, even 50 years after.
